coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode nanoprobe for selective detection of arsenite based on yellow fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Lei
Yuan, Lin
Xiang, Yingying
Song, Fei
Jiang, Wenjun
Ji, Xiuling
Tu, Yujiao
coredata.dc:description: Arsenic is a toxic heavy metal element and widely distributed in nature, however, long-term exposure to low doses of arsenite (As3+) is harmful to human health. Therefore, it is particularly important to establish a quick and sensitive detection method for As3+ in the environment. Yellow fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal method with rhodamine B and citrate as precursors. The RbB-CDs were purified by thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column. The good line ranges of 0–5 μg/L and 10–50 μg/L As3+ were obtained with low detection limits of 0.75 μg/L and 5.00 μg/L based on fluorescence, respectively, while a good line range of 0–50 μg/L was obtained with a low detection limit of 3.03 μg/L based on UV absorbance. Furthermore, static quenching was verified to work in the quenching process of As3+ towards RbB-CDs fluorescence. In addition, the yellow fluorescent nanoprobe was employed to determine the As3+ content in tap water, lake water, and river water samples. It showed good potential to detect As3+ in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rhodamine B
As3+
Static quenching
Carbon dots
Dual mode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime measurement
Possible quenching mechanisms of As3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (inset: HR-TEM) and (b) FTIR spectrum (c) XRD pattern of RbB-CDs.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of the crude RbB-CDs. High-resolution C1s spectra (b), N1s spectra (c) and O1s spectra (d).
(a) UV–vis, excitation, emission spectra, (b) Excitation and emission spectra of RbB-CDs.

Fluorescence quenching efficiency of metal ions and standard solutions (a), l-amino acids (b) to the crude RbB-CDs. Fluorescence quenching efficiency of metal ions (c) to the secondary purified RbB-CDs.

Fluorescence (a) and UV absorbance (c) spectra of RbB-CDs with As3+ (0–50 μg/L) concentrations. The Line relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency of the purified RbB-CDs and As3+ concentrations (0–5 μg/mL, 10–50 μg/L) (b). The Line relationship between UV absorbance rate of the purified RbB-CDs and As3+ (0–50 μg/L) (d).
UV–vis absorption of As3+, RbB-CDs, RbB-CDs + As3+ (a) and decay lifetime of RbB-CDs, RbB-CDs + As3+ (b). UV–vis absorption of As3+ and Ex, Em of RbB-CDs (c).
Comparison of detection performance of As3+.

row.entry: Edible prickly pear cactus fruit, GSH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of As3+ determination in real samples by RhB-CDs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Dual-mode nanoprobe for selective detection of arsenite based on yellow fluorescent carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Arsenic is a toxic heavy metal element and widely distributed in nature, however, long-term exposure to low doses of arsenite (As3+) is harmful to human health. Therefore, it is particularly important to establish a quick and sensitive detection method for As3+ in the environment. Yellow fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal method with rhodamine B and citrate as precursors. The RbB-CDs were purified by thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column. The good line ranges of 0–5 μg/L and 10–50 μg/L As3+ were obtained with low detection limits of 0.75 μg/L and 5.00 μg/L based on fluorescence, respectively, while a good line range of 0–50 μg/L was obtained with a low detection limit of 3.03 μg/L based on UV absorbance. Furthermore, static quenching was verified to work in the quenching process of As3+ towards RbB-CDs fluorescence. In addition, the yellow fluorescent nanoprobe was employed to determine the As3+ content in tap water, lake water, and river water samples. It showed good potential to detect As3+ in the environment.

Schematic of the synthetic and purification route for yellow fluorescent RhB-CDs and the applications in detection of As3+.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution has become an urgent problem and threatened the living environment and human health. Among them, heavy metal pollution is one of the most important forms. Arsenic is a toxic heavy metal element and is widely distributed in nature. Arsenic contamination could enter the biosphere by the food chain when dissolved in water. Usually, arsenic is present in four forms, such as arsenite (As3+), arsenate (As5+), monomethylarsonic acid (MMAV), and dimethylarsinic acid (DMAV). Especially, As3+ and As5+ are the main forms and show an important effect on the survival of human , owing to entering the food cycle (drinking water, rice, seafood) and accumulating in living organisms [1–5]. As3+ is often bound to sulfhydryl and thiol groups of enzymes or proteins, therefore, the change in their structure and function disrupts cellular respiration and the production of energy [6,7]. World Health Organization (WHO) reports that the concentration of arsenic in drinking water >10 ppb is highly unsafe for human health [8]. It shows that long-term exposure to low doses of As3+ is associated with cancers, even death [9–12]. Therefore, it is particularly important to establish a quick and sensitive detection method for As3+ in the environment.

Currently, a lot of techniques for detecting the toxicity of As3+, such as Raman spectroscopy (RS) [13], atomic absorption spectrometry [14], HPLC-inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (HPLC-ICP-MS) [15], the fontal chromatography-ICP-MS [16], electrochemical biosensors [17], total reflection X-ray fluorescence [18], and laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy [19]. All of these techniques are susceptible and accurate to detect As3+. But the instrument-based techniques require highly skilled labor, long operating time, and a managed lab environment. Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn more and more attention and have been employed for environmental monitoring, such as detecting heavy metal ions, environmental pollution, and as drug delivery vectors [20–26]. Thus, an economical and simple detection method of As3+ based on fluorescent CDs could be an effective alternative. In this work, yellow fluorescent CDs (Rb-CDs) were prepared with Rhodamine B and citrate as precursors. Furthermore, the nanoprobe RbB-CDs were applied for As3+ detection in real samples.

All chemical reagents were provided by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), including rhodamine B (RbB), l-glutamate, citrate (CA), l-proline, AgNO3, l-cysteine, ethanol, l-tryptophan, FeCl3, l-glycine, BaCl2, l-aspartic acid, CoCl2, l-lysine, acetone, l-glutamine, Bi, l-serine, Ni(NO3)2, l-threonine, methanol, l-valine, dimethylformamide (DMF), l-isoleucine, CuSO4, l-arginine, HgCl2, l-leucine, KCl, l-methionine, Pb(NO3)2, l-phenylalanine, isopropanol, l-tyrosine, MgCl2, l-histidine, CaCl2, and l-alanine. Standard solutions were purchased from Xiamen Haibao Technology Co., Ltd. (Xiamen, China), such as nitrate, sulfate, fluorine, nitrite, bicarbonate, chlorine, iodine, acetate, bromine, carbonate, and phosphate.

Vacuum freeze-drying system (CHRIST ALPHA 1–4, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance). G9800A fluorospectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Spectrofluorometer (FS5, England). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) Spectrometer (Bruker AM-400).

Briefly, 0.5 g RhB and 1.0 g CA dissolved in 20 mL ethanol, and transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene high temperature reactor at 200 °C for 8 h. After the natural cooling, the crude RbB-CDs were characterized with FTIR, XPS and XRD.

The crude RbB-CDs were submitted to silica gel column chromatography and then eluted with gradient petroleum ether and acetone from 1:1 to 1:5. Directed by thin layer chromatography, the elution with yellow fluorescence was collected and concentrated under vacuum to obtain the primary purified RhB-CDs, then characterized with TEM.

The primary purified RbB-CDs were submitted to silica gel column chromatography and eluted with gradient CH2Cl2 and MeOH from 10:1 to 2:1. Directed by thin layer chromatography, the secondary purified RhB-CDs with yellow fluorescence were obtained and detected by NMR spectrometer.

The preparation conditions of RbB-CDs were optimization, including different solvents (DMF, ethanol, methanol, acetone, and isopropanol), proportions of RbB and CA (nRbB/nCA = 1:10, 1:5, 1:3.33, 1:2.5, and 1:2), reaction temperatures (170–220 °C), and reaction times (2–12 h). Quantum yield (QY) was measured.

The selectivity of RbB-CDs towards different metal ions, amino acids, and standard solutions at 1 mM was investigated. Typically, 3 μL RbB-CDs solution and 997 μL phosphate buffer solution (1 mM) were mixed, adding with different concentrations of As3+ solution and diluted to 4.0 mL with sterile water. The fluorescence intensity was measured after 2 min at the slit width of 2.5 nm. The selectivity of As3+ and other substances was performed. To estimate the coexisting interference, the effect of 1 mM A3+ on the fluorescence intensity of RbB-CDs was analyzed at different pHs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
QY value of quinine-sulfate was 54% at excitation 360 nm for fluorescence measurement when dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, and was appointed as a reference:
Q
C D s
=
Q R
(
I
C D s
/
I R
)
(
A R
/
A
C D s
)
(
η
C D s
2
/
η R 2
)

Two following equations are used to calculate fluorescence lifetime:
τ =
∑
A n
τ n
2
A n
τ n
( n = 1,2,3 … … )
Y = B +
A 1
* exp
( − i /
τ 1
)
+ … +
A n
* exp
( − i /
τ n
)
( n = 1,2,3 … … )

To study the actual probability of RbB-CDs, water samples were obtained from tap water, Tingxin lake water, and Baoxiang river water. All samples were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrance for As3+ determination. The 3.8 mg RbB-CDs were dissolved in 10 mL MeOH. The 3 μL RbB-CDs solution was diluted to 2 mL with ddH2O, and the fluorescence was measured as F0. Then, 3 μL RbB-CDs solution was diluted to 2 mL with treated water samples, and the fluorescence was measured as F. Meantime, spiking experiment as follows: As3+ standard solution with different volumes was added to the treated samples containing 3 μL RbB-CDs, then the recoveries of As3+ were determined.

Yellow crude RbB-CDs solutions were obtained via a hydrothermal method and then were purified with dialysis, thin layer chromatography, and chromatographic column. When the optimization conditions were as followings: ethanol as the selected solvent, nRbB/ncitrate = 1:3.33, reaction temperature 200 °C, and reaction times 8 h, the QY value of RbB-CDs reached the maximum (Fig. S1). No signals were found in the 1HNMR and 13CNMR of the purified RhB-CDs (Fig. S2), indicating the carbon nanocapsules without molecular structure.

The morphological characteristics and the particle size distribution of RbB-CDs were investigated by TEM. The average diameter of RbB-CDs was about 1.526 nm with uniform spherical particles, and the lattice spacing was about 0.222 nm (Fig. 1 a). The surface chemistry characteristics of RbB-CDs were assessed by FTIR. The absorption peaks presented at 3465.32 cm−1, 2938.99 cm−1, 1735.63 cm−1, 1623.77 cm−1, 1375 cm−1, and 1201.44 cm−1, attributing to O–H bond, the weak C–H bond, strong CO bond, CC bond, C–H bond and C–N bond, respectively (Fig. 1b). The XRD was used to exhibit the crystallinity of RbB-CDs, and the 2θ value was 21.27 (Fig. 1c).

C1s, N1s, and O1s correspond to the peaks at 284.8 eV, 401.2 eV, and 532.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 a). Three peaks presented at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, and 289.0 eV, attributing to CC, C–O, and CO in the C1s spectrum, respectively (Fig. 2b). Two peaks at 399.4 and 401.4 eV correspond to C–N and N–H bonds in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c). In the O1s spectrum, two peaks presented at 532.3 eV and 533.8 eV, attributing to C–H–O/O–C–O and CO, respectively (Fig. 2d). Therefore, RbB-CDs showed good water solubility with rich hydrophilic and amino functional groups.

Two peaks around 256 nm and 282 nm, correspond to π-π* transitions, and one peak around 308 nm, corresponds to n-π* transition (Fig. 3 a). The emission and optimal excitation of RbB-CDs was 580 nm and 360 nm, respectively (Fig. 3b). When excitation shifted from 330 to 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity enhanced, and then lowered. A red shift occurs indicating wavelength dependent.
RbB-CDs fluorescence stability was performed. 7.0 was the optimal pH (Fig. S3a).

RbB-CDs showed slight fluctuation within NaCl 0–2.0 M concentration (Fig. S3b). A slight variation of the fluorescence intensity was presented after adding 0–100 mM H2O2, showing the good antioxidant performance (Fig. S3c). No significant change in fluorescence quenching efficiency was found under natural (Fig. S3d) and UV (Fig. S3e) light, indicating RbB-CDs had strong photostability and stability under UV.

The QY values of the crude and purified RbB-CDs were 26.39% and 28.19%, respectively, based on quinine-sulfate for fluorescence measurement (QY = 54%). Due to the high photostability compared to other fluorescent dyes, RhB was chosen as a precursor. As a cheap and easy industrial raw material, citrate was used as a nitrogen doping for CDs synthesis, which can increase the QY of CDs.

The selectivity of RbB-CDs was monitored. Only As3+ could quench the yellow fluorescence with high efficiency (Fig. 4 a and b). There was no interference between the other metal ions (Fig. 4c). Therefore, RbB-CDs could be as a nanoprobe to detect As3+.

The effect of pH on RbB-CDs fluorescence intensity was measured within pH 2.2–12 after adding As3+. The maximum quenching efficiency was obtained at pH 6.0 (Fig. S4a). The 2 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time (Fig. S4b).

Due to suspension in solution, the concentration and aggregation form of RhB-CDs may be different, then the light scattering was different, causing the concentration of As3+ not show a linear relationship with (F0–F)/F0 in some areas, thus the gap presents between the two ranges. The phenomenon was similar to other reports [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanisms of As3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, there are five main quenching mechanisms of the quencher to the fluorescence of CDs, including static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner filter effect (IFE) [43].

UV–vis absorption was analyzed to study the quenching mechanism of As3+ to the RbB-CDs yellow fluorescence (Fig. 6 a). Variation of the absorption of RbB-CDs was observed with or without As3+. Therefore, the SQ process may be worked.
No overlap existed between the RbB-CDs excitation spectrum and the As3+ absorption spectrum (Fig. 6b). IFE was excluded in As3+ quenching the fluorescence.
No change in the decay lifetime fluorescence was observed without (1.78 ns) or with As3+ (1.73 ns) (Fig. 6c). Therefore, DQ, PET, and FRET were excluded [43].

Stern-Volmer equation was used to verify SQ [43]:
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]

The slope (0.03871, 0.03795, 0.03109) and Kq (2.21 × 1010 M−1‧S−1, 2.16 × 1010 M−1‧S−1, 1.77 × 1010 M−1‧S−1) decreased when the temperature (288 K, 298 K, 310 K) raised (Fig. S5), thus SQ worked. Based on UV–vis absorption, τ / τ
0 = 1, and the effects of temperature on Kq, the quenching mechanism of As3+ towards RbB-CDs fluorescence was verified to be SQ.

The content of As3+ in tap water, lake water, and river water was detected, showing the sensitivity of RbB-CDs on As3+ content determination (n = 3). The recovery rate of As3+ with RbB-CDs was 101.4–104.4% based on fluorescence (Table 2 ). Due to simple and rapid synthesis, RbB-CDs yellow fluorescent nanoprobe was suitable for the detection of As3+ in real samples.

The yellow fluorescent RbB-CDs were prepared with RbB and CA as the precursor. It showed excellent fluorescence features and was employed to detect As3+ with excellent linear ranges and LOD based on UV and fluorescence dual mode. In addition, the nanoprobe was applied to determine As3+ in tap water, lake water, and river water samples. Thus, RbB-CDs showed great potential for detecting As3+ in the environment.

Lei Jiang: Designed the experiment, Writing – original draft. Lin Yuan: Designed the experiment, Writing – original draft. Yingying Xiang: Methodology. Fei Song: Methodology. Wenjun Jiang: Data curation. Xiuling Ji: Writing – original draft. Yujiao Tu: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work funded by Science and Technology Planning Project of Yunnan Science and Technology Department Joint Project of Local Colleges and Universities (202001BA070001-218).
